p.a:
This work is licensed under a CC
Attribution 4.0 International License

presentation.title: OpenClaw & Universal Agents: Why API-First Is Now an Agent Requirement
presentation.author: Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Architecture at Jentic
Governance Board Member at the OpenAPI Initiative
Co-Maintainer of the Arazzo Specification
Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)

img.style: float: right ; height : 60% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

ol.li: MCP is an entry, but it's really APIs all the way down
OpenClaw prefers direct API / CLI access - the shortest path

ul.li: Don't break consumers: Design a contract and stick to it
Speed up delivery: API providers and consumers work in parallel
API products: Design APIs instead of exposing implementations
Scalable Governance: Implementation-independent APIs live longer

slide.title: Optionality: Unbundling the Enterprise with APIs

img.title: Stephen Fishman and Matt McLarty, "Unbundling the Enterprise: APIs, Optionality, and the Science of Happy Accidents", 2024
img.style: float: right ; height : 70% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

ul.li: Build up capabilities for experimentation and discovery
APIs are the foundational layer of digital capabilities
API First builds everything as digital building blocks
Optionality is a potent accelerant for AI success
2000s: Having any API is good
2010s: Having many APIs is good
2020s: Having many developer-friendly APIs is good
2025: Having many machine-friendly APIs is good

slide.title: API First 2.0: From DX to AX

ul.li: AI needs capabilities: AI cannot do anything without APIs → ✅
Runtime discovery/usage: Find and use APIs when needed → 🎥
Workflows: Make workflows part of the API strategy → 🎥
Agent risk management: Limit blast radius with APIs → 🎥
Sandboxes: Create a safe space for agents to experiment in → 🎥
Most API descriptions need quality improvements anyway
Better descriptions benefit everyone, not just AI agents
Complete and meaningful operation summaries
Comprehensive examples for all operations
Clear error handling
Consistent terminology and naming
Good DX is the foundation for good AX
AI agents need information developers don't typically require:
Everything that developers usually just know
Treat every API like an external one for developers with no context
OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) might add AX-specific fields
Until then, leverage description fields strategically
Discoverable: able to find and classify API
Interpretable: intent must be explicit
Composable: clear inputs / outputs
Safe: authNZ & safety signals machine readable
Predictable: idempotency, stable responses
Resilient: clear signals for success and failure

slide.title: You Can't Improve What You Can't Measure
slide.p: AI-readiness starts with understanding your current API landscape

slide.title: API AI-Readiness Framework
slide.p: Evaluates APIs across six human & AI-readiness dimensions

ul.li: Evaluates if an API is structurally valid, standards-compliant, and parseable by tooling.

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;

tr.td: Checks whether the API parses as a valid OpenAPI
What proportion of $ref references successfully resolve
Aggregated quality score from linter diagnostics, weighted by severity
Measures whether the API's underlying data model is coherent enough for automated reasoning (semantic or logical defects that impact reliable interpretation)

li.code: structural_integrity = max(0, 1 - log10(1 + structural_issues) / log10(1 + structural_issue_threshold))

ul.li: Evaluates how easily AI systems can locate, classify, and route to the API across registries, workflows, and knowledge bases.

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;

tr.td: Evaluates the semantic value of textual descriptions within an API description
Evaluates verb-object clarity of summaries and descriptions
Evaluates indicators to workflow specifics including Arazzo/MCP/workflow references. Also analyses existence of callbacks etc.
Detects metadata related to API gateways, llms.txt, APIs.json, MCP registries, externalDocs links to Dev portals
Detect domain/taxonomy classification through the use of tags

slide.title: API Discoverability - Do it for the Agents (and your teams)!

ul.li: Better Signal Scores = Better APIs = Better Searchability
Improved results in vanilla and specialized search offerings
Using expensive models doesn't fix bad API semantics
Save yourself the trouble and the $$$

img.title: You May Have OpenAPI, But Is It AI-Ready - from APIDays Paris Dec 2025

slide.title: AI Enablement: Search, Workflows, Security, Sandboxes
slide.p: “Endpoints != Use-cases. Think about the workflows” - Adam DuVander

presentation.title: Agent Skills, MCP, and Workflows: Tools for Enabling Agents
presentation.author: Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde
presentation.toc: MCP Spec · Agent Skills Spec · Arazzo Spec · Jentic Scorecard

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Architecture at Jentic
Governance Board Member at the OpenAPI Initiative
Co-Maintainer of the Arazzo Specification
Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)
Agents are evolving from single-purpose tools to broad-capability systems
Each new capability adds tools — and decisions — to the agent's scope

li.em: More capability → more decisions → more room for error

ul.li: The question is not whether agents can do more. It's how to make them do it reliably.
Tool overload: too many options degrade agent decision quality
Tool selection is itself a reasoning task — and it fails at scale
Three patterns address this directly: Agent Skills, MCP, and Arazzo Workflows
We will use a single scenario throughout: Your API has just landed in your repo. How do you ensure it is AI-ready?

slide.title: The Reproducibility Ladder
slide.p: Four approaches to agent capability — increasing determinism at each rung:

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;

slide.p: * LLMs can also author and register Arazzo workflows — converting observed processes into reusable, deterministic assets stored for future agent use.

ul.li: Agents are software that needs maintenance
Agents run on LLMs which make them powerful and shape-shifting
The same inputs produce different outputs when the model changes
Keeping the model stable may be difficult or impossible
Evals are good and useful but very hard to do for universal agents
MCP is the current standard protocol for LLM consumers
The initial focus was on additional and extended context
Additional context by providing access to the environment
Extended context by allowing MCP servers to externally manage context
Many mapping tools generate MCP servers from OpenAPI descriptions
What to expose and how to design it is out of scope

ol.li: Publish/Install MCP servers for all APIs
Consumers can access all MCP tools

slide.title: MCP all the APIs: Easy but Limited

ul.li: Zero development effort, many tools auto-generate MCP
All APIs easily available
Great for quick and focused experimentation
Context window overflow happens quickly
AI struggles with understanding workflows across endpoints
Risk of misinterpreting MCP problems as general AI problems
Good as a starting point but lacks sophistication and scalability

slide.title: What Is an Agent Skill?

ul.li: Structured, reusable context for a specific subtask
Lives in the LLM's context window — shapes how it thinks and acts
Explicit scope: does one thing, with guardrails, in a defined way
Versioned artifact — reviewed, shared, and reused across agents

slide.p: At this rung: We load the jentic-openapi-audit skill. The LLM knows exactly what tool to call, what format to produce, what not to do. Consistent output. Reusable across teams.

slide.title: Anatomy of a Skill
slide.p: A SKILL.md defines: task · constraints · steps · references to external scripts or tools

ul.li: Task: what the skill is for — clear, bounded scope
Constraints: what the LLM must and must not do
Steps: how to approach the task, in order
References: external scripts, tools, or MCP capabilities to invoke

slide.p: Versioned, shareable, and loadable by any compatible agent. Think of it as a reusable prompt with structure and guardrails baked in.

p.q: A skill tells the LLM how to think about a task. A skill can discover and trigger a workflow — once it does, the LLM is out of the execution loop.

ul.li: Use when: recurring subtask needing LLM judgment · consistent output required · reusability matters
Not for: multi-step processes with a defined sequence · capabilities needed by multiple systems
Reproducibility: Medium — guided, not guaranteed
A skill can trigger a workflow. Once triggered, the LLM is out of the execution loop.
Same prompt, three runs, three outcomes. Token waste. No audit trail.
At one agent, a bug. At a hundred, a governance crisis.
As repeatable processes multiply, so does the server catalog
You have traded LLM unpredictability for infrastructure maintenance

p.em: Can you describe the process once, run it identically every time, and not maintain a server to do it?

ul.li: Declarative: define the sequence, data flow, and success criteria in a single document
The OpenAPI Initiative standard for multi-step API workflow descriptions
Arazzo workflows reference operationIds from OpenAPI descriptions (soon also AsyncAPI descriptions)
Discoverable by agents (via MCP or as APIs): find and trigger a workflow without knowing its internals
Use when: multi-step process · defined sequence · auditability required · enterprise or regulated context
Not for: exploratory tasks · tasks needing LLM judgment at each step
Reproducibility: High

slide.title: Should You Build an Agent? Now Answer It.
slide.p: Based on Anthropic's Should I Build an Agent? framework — extended with the full toolkit:

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;

tr.td: No → Arazzo. Yes but needs context → Skill first, leveraging MCP tools as needed; once the path is known, graduate to Arazzo.
Low-cost repeatable → Arazzo. Needs LLM judgment each time → Skill.
Are all steps known and fixed?
Yes → Arazzo. Context-dependent → Skill guides, Arazzo executes.
What is the cost of error?
High → Arazzo with decision points. Shared capability → MCP.

p.em: The question was never just agent vs. workflow. It was: what level of determinism does this task actually need?

slide.title: How They Layer: The Full Picture

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: center;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: center;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: center;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: center;

slide.p: Key distinction: A skill tells the LLM how to think about a task (flexible path). A workflow tells the system what to do, step by step, without asking the LLM (fixed path). They compose: skills discover and trigger workflows.

ol.li: Score your current APIs — know how ready they are to enable agents before your agents find out the hard way
Identify your capabilities — where are you on the ladder? What level of reproducibility does each task actually need?
Ask: who else needs this capability? — if it's agents only, Arazzo may be enough; if humans, other systems, and agents all need it, you need more than just MCP
Highest-risk recurring task → write a skill for it
Capability needed by multiple systems → wrap it in MCP
Multi-step business process → describe it in Arazzo
